title: Subordinate Clauses (Nebensatz)
description: Subordinate clauses (Nebensätze) are dependent clauses introduced by a conjunction such as 'weil', 'dass', 'wenn', or 'ob'. In these clauses, the conjugated verb moves to the very end. A comma always separates the subordinate clause from the main clause. Mastering Nebensätze is a key milestone in German grammar.
whenToUse: When expressing that-clauses or reported speech (dass)
When describing conditions or habitual actions (wenn)
When expressing doubt or indirect yes/no questions (ob)
When referring to past events (als), or sequencing events (bevor, nachdem)

rules.title: Verb Goes to the End
rules.explanation: In a subordinate clause, the conjugated verb moves to the final position. This is the defining feature of all Nebensätze and applies regardless of which conjunction introduces the clause.

examples.correct: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin.
examples.incorrect: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich bin krank.
examples.explanation: After 'weil', the conjugated verb 'bin' must go to the end of the clause.

examples.correct: Er sagt, dass er morgen kommt.
examples.incorrect: Er sagt, dass er kommt morgen.
examples.explanation: In the dass-clause, 'kommt' moves to the final position, after 'morgen'.

examples.correct: Ich weiß nicht, ob sie heute Abend Zeit hat.
examples.explanation: The verb 'hat' goes to the very end of the ob-clause.

rules.title: Comma Before the Conjunction
rules.explanation: A comma is always required before the subordinating conjunction that introduces the Nebensatz. This is a strict punctuation rule in German, unlike in English where commas with subordinate clauses are often optional.

examples.correct: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Berlin arbeiten möchte.
examples.incorrect: Ich lerne Deutsch weil ich in Berlin arbeiten möchte.
examples.explanation: A comma must appear before 'weil' to separate the main clause from the subordinate clause.

rules.title: Nebensatz First — Verb-Subject Inversion in Main Clause
rules.explanation: When the subordinate clause comes before the main clause, it occupies position 1 of the overall sentence. The main clause then starts with its conjugated verb in position 2, followed by the subject (inversion). The pattern is: Nebensatz + comma + Verb + Subject + rest.

examples.correct: Wenn es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause.
examples.incorrect: Wenn es regnet, ich bleibe zu Hause.
examples.explanation: The entire wenn-clause fills position 1, so the main clause must begin with the verb 'bleibe' (position 2), then the subject 'ich'.

examples.correct: Obwohl er müde war, ging er joggen.
examples.explanation: After the obwohl-clause, the main clause inverts: verb 'ging' before subject 'er'.
The bevor-clause comes first, so the main clause starts with the verb 'dusche' followed by subject 'ich'.

rules.title: Separable Verbs Rejoin in the Nebensatz
rules.explanation: In a main clause, separable verbs split apart. In a subordinate clause, the prefix re-attaches to the verb, and the whole verb goes to the end as one word.

examples.correct: Ich weiß, dass der Zug um 8 Uhr abfährt.
examples.incorrect: Ich weiß, dass der Zug um 8 Uhr fährt ab.
examples.explanation: In the Nebensatz, the separable verb 'abfahren' stays together as 'abfährt' at the end.

examples.correct: Er ist traurig, weil seine Freundin nicht anruft.
examples.explanation: 'Anrufen' stays as one word 'anruft' at the end of the weil-clause.

rules.title: Modal Verbs and Perfect Tense in Nebensatz
rules.explanation: When a subordinate clause contains a modal verb + infinitive or a perfect tense construction, the conjugated verb still goes to the very end, after the infinitive or past participle.

examples.correct: Ich weiß, dass er morgen arbeiten muss.
examples.incorrect: Ich weiß, dass er morgen muss arbeiten.
examples.explanation: The modal verb 'muss' (conjugated) goes after the infinitive 'arbeiten' — to the very end.

examples.correct: Sie ist froh, weil sie die Prüfung bestanden hat.
examples.explanation: In the perfect tense Nebensatz, the auxiliary 'hat' goes to the final position, after the past participle 'bestanden'.

examples.correct: Er fragt, ob ich ihm helfen kann.
examples.explanation: The modal 'kann' follows the infinitive 'helfen' at the end of the ob-clause.

tables.title: Common Subordinating Conjunctions
rows: Ich bleibe hier, weil es regnet.
Wenn ich Zeit habe, lese ich.
Ich weiß nicht, ob er kommt.
Er geht raus, obwohl es kalt ist.
Als ich klein war, spielte ich viel.
Bevor du gehst, ruf mich an.
Nachdem er gegessen hatte, ging er.
Er rannte schnell, sodass er den Bus erreichte.

commonMistakes.correct: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich bin krank.
commonMistakes.explanation: In a subordinate clause the conjugated verb must go to the end. 'bin' must come after 'krank'.

commonMistakes.correct: Wenn es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Wenn es regnet, ich bleibe zu Hause.
commonMistakes.explanation: When the Nebensatz comes first, the main clause must start with the verb (inversion). 'bleibe' before 'ich'.

commonMistakes.correct: Ich weiß, dass der Zug um 8 Uhr abfährt.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Ich weiß, dass der Zug um 8 Uhr fährt ab.
commonMistakes.explanation: Separable verbs rejoin in a Nebensatz. The prefix does not separate — write 'abfährt' as one word at the end.

commonMistakes.correct: Sie sagt, dass sie kommen kann.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Sie sagt, dass sie kann kommen.
commonMistakes.explanation: With modal verbs in a Nebensatz, the modal (conjugated) goes after the infinitive at the very end.

commonMistakes.correct: Ich bin müde, weil ich nicht geschlafen habe.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Ich bin müde, weil ich habe nicht geschlafen.
commonMistakes.explanation: In perfect tense subordinate clauses, the auxiliary 'habe' must go to the final position.

commonMistakes.correct: Er rief an, bevor er kam.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Er rief an bevor er kam.
commonMistakes.explanation: A comma is always required before a subordinating conjunction in German.

tips: When you see a subordinating conjunction (weil, dass, wenn, ob, etc.), immediately move the conjugated verb to the end of that clause.
If your sentence starts with a Nebensatz, remember the main clause flips to Verb-Subject order. Think of it as: the entire Nebensatz is sitting in position 1.
Separable verbs become 'normal' again in a Nebensatz — the prefix and verb stem stick together as one word at the end.
Practice by taking simple Hauptsätze and converting them: 'Ich bin müde' becomes '…, weil ich müde bin.'
Watch out for 'weil' in spoken German — many native speakers use main clause word order after 'weil' in casual speech, but this is considered incorrect in writing.
